ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general barley beverage class; no specific entry for home-made Southeast Asian barley water (薏米水 / Yi Mi Shui). Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers barley-based beverages as a category. No specific code exists for the traditional Southeast Asian sweetened barley water preparation. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept identified for home-made barley water as a food item.

preparation_method: sweetened with rock sugar or pandan

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one cup or glass), as commonly served in Malaysian and Singaporean hawker stalls and restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Barley water, sweetened — IMR Malaysia MyFCD / FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central barley grain data for estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia; FAO/INFOODS; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Home-made barley water (薏米水) is a dilute grain beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entry for sweetened barley water and cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA grain beverage data. The drink is predominantly water with a small amount of dissolved starch, sugars from rock sugar, and minimal protein and fat leached from the grain during boiling. Values represent a lightly sweetened preparation (approximately 5–8 g sugar per 100 ml) as commonly prepared at home and in hawker settings.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of barley-based foods and beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; Liljeberg H, Björck I. Delayed gastric emptying rate may explain improved glycaemia in healthy subjects to a starchy meal with added vinegar. Eur J Clin Nutr. 1998; supplemented by Atkinson FS et al. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; European Journal of Clinical Nutrition; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for home-made Southeast Asian barley water (薏米水). GI estimated at 25 based on: (1) pearl barley grain GI of 22–28 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; (2) the beverage form is a dilute aqueous extract with substantially lower starch content than whole grain, supporting a GI at the lower end of the barley range; (3) added rock sugar (sucrose) contributes modest glycemic load but the overall carbohydrate concentration per 100 ml is low (~6 g). GL per 250 ml serving = (25 × 6.0 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.75, rounded to 3.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~2 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Home-made barley water is very low in sodium (~5 mg per 250 ml serving) and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension; however, commercially prepared versions may contain added salt or preservatives and should be checked.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~25) and low glycemic load per serving (~3.8)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Home-made barley water has a low GI (~25) and low GL (~3.8 per 250 ml serving), making it a suitable beverage choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when prepared with minimal added sugar; individuals should monitor total carbohydrate intake if sweetened versions are consumed regularly.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; beta-glucan from barley may support lipid management
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Home-made barley water contains negligible saturated fat and cholesterol, and the beta-glucan soluble fibre leached from barley during boiling may contribute modestly to LDL cholesterol reduction, supporting cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Home-made barley water (薏米水, Yi Mi Shui) is a traditional Southeast Asian grain beverage widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore. It is prepared by boiling pearl barley (Coix lacryma-jobi, also known as Job's tears or coix seed) in water, then straining the liquid and sweetening it lightly with rock sugar and sometimes pandan leaves for fragrance. The resulting drink is pale, slightly cloudy, mildly sweet, and has a subtle earthy, nutty flavour characteristic of barley. It is served both hot and cold and is a staple at Malaysian and Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and homes. Nutritionally, barley water is a low-calorie, low-fat beverage with approximately 25 kcal per 100 ml, predominantly from dissolved carbohydrates and added sugar. It contains trace amounts of protein, B vitamins, potassium, magnesium, and phosphorus leached from the grain during boiling. The beverage has a low glycemic index (~25) and low glycemic load, making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Beta-glucan soluble fibre from barley may contribute modest cardiovascular and glycaemic benefits. In traditional Chinese medicine, 薏米 (yi mi) is regarded as having cooling and diuretic properties. The drink is naturally gluten-containing (barley), halal, vegan, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a refreshing daily beverage, particularly during warm weather, and is believed to support skin health and reduce internal heat in traditional wellness contexts.
common_user_queries: Is barley water good for diabetes?
How many calories in barley water?
Can I drink barley water if I have high blood pressure?
How is home-made barley water prepared?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source provides a direct nutrient entry for home-made Southeast Asian barley water (薏米水) as a prepared beverage. Values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA grain beverage data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central barley grain composition. The GI is an analogous estimate based on pearl barley GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a lightly sweetened home-made preparation (~5–8 g sugar per 100 ml). Unsweetened versions will have lower energy_kcal and sugar_g. Heavily sweetened commercial versions may have significantly higher sugar and energy values. 2. protein_g (0.2) and fat_g (0.1) are very low but non-zero, reflecting trace leaching from barley grain during boiling; these are not zero values and require no zero-justification phrase. 3. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg are reported as 0.0 — confirmed zero or effectively zero per IMR Malaysia MyFCD and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for this beverage type; these are confirmed zero values. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this beverage. 5. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this beverage. 6. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this beverage. 7. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this beverage. 8. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source databases for this beverage. 9. glycemic_index is set to 25 (low category) based on analogous estimate from pearl barley GI data per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for this specific beverage preparation exists in approved sources. 10. The food is classified as Malaysian cuisine but is equally common in Singapore; multilingual names cover English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean/Malaysian dish requirements. 11. Barley (Hordeum vulgare / Coix lacryma-jobi) contains gluten; this beverage is not suitable for individuals with coeliac disease or gluten intolerance. Note: in Singapore/Malaysia, 薏米 (yi mi) typically refers to Coix lacryma-jobi (Job's tears), which is technically a different grain from common barley (Hordeum vulgare) and is gluten-free; however, the English name 'barley water' and the field survey listing context suggest the common pearl barley preparation. This ambiguity is noted and the allergen flag reflects the common barley (Hordeum vulgare) interpretation. If the dish uses Coix lacryma-jobi exclusively, it would be gluten-free.
